abstract: Extracting structured knowledge from the vast body of unstructured scientific literature is a critical challenge for accelerating materials discovery. While traditional natural language processing (NLP) methods often focus on named entity recognition (NER), capturing the complex, hierarchical relationships between entities—such as the link between a specific chemical composition, its morphology, and its resulting properties—remains difficult. Here, we present a flexible approach to joint NER and relation extraction (RE) by fine-tuning pre-trained large language models (LLMs), specifically GPT-3 and Llama-2. We demonstrate that these models can be trained with relatively few human-annotated examples (approximately 100–600) to extract sophisticated scientific records and output them in structured formats like JSON or natural language. Our method was evaluated across three distinct materials chemistry tasks: identifying host-dopant relationships, cataloging metal-organic frameworks (MOFs), and general materials information extraction. The results show that LLMs outperform traditional BERT-based pipelines in capturing complex relations, with the added benefit of providing built-in entity normalization and error correction. Furthermore, we show that using partially trained models in a human-in-the-loop workflow can reduce manual annotation time by over 50%. This approach offers an accessible and powerful framework for researchers to build specialized, high-fidelity databases from scientific text with minimal NLP expertise.
